displayTitle: 2026-05-13 - Māori Affairs Committee - Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill
topic: Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill
description: Wednesday, 13 May 2026 | Māori Affairs Committee Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill Hearing of evidence Submitters List: Te Wairoa Tapokorau Mai Tawhiti Trust Paoraian Manuel-Harman Paul Harman Charles Lambert Erepeti Marae Committee Tina Mihaere, Secretary for Erepeti Marae Committee Ngati Pahauwera Development Trust Tatau Tatau o Te Wairoa Trust Pieri Munro, MNZM, Chair of Tātau Tātau o Te Wairoa Trust Te Whare Taupoki
descriptionSummary: Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill. Te Wairoa Tapokorau Mai Tawhiti Trust. Paoraian Manuel-Harman

chunks.text: We've just been going through with some readings in the House. But I welcome you to this hearing as well. So we'll formally reopen the meeting itself. Thank you for the submission that you've provided. We've had an opportunity to have a brief read of that. We've got 10 minutes allocated for your time. We're going to be quite strict because the bells will be ringing towards the end there. I want everybody to have the opportunity to get through theirs as well.

So you use your 10 minutes as you see fit. If you can leave some time at the end there for some questions, if we have them. So kia ora mai. Ka pai. Tēnā koe e te chair mana. Tēnā koutou e nga mima o tēnei komiti. Ko Paul Emmanuel Harmon tōku ingoa. To my left here is Paul Harmon, our legal counsel, and at the end there is Uncle Charles Lambert. I appear today not only as a descendant of Whānau Hapū uri o Te Rohe o Te Wairoa, but also as co-chairperson of Te Wairoa Te Pokoroī Mai Tawhiti Trust, Cluster One.

The kāhui trust that represents Whānau Hapū, iwi and uri who signed alongside other kāhui within tātou tātou o Te Wairoa settlement. On their behalf, we say to you, we cannot let this bill pass in its current form, as we contest the transfer of six stock reserves listed in Schedule 2 of this bill. Mangaone, Panekirikiri, Tutaemaro, Waihi South, Waikariiti, Ruakituri Reserve, located in what is referred to as the four southern blocks, and to the ownership of Te Uruwera Board.

We want to be clear that we acknowledge and support Ngāti Ruapani and all Māori treaty claims and cultural redress to date and in future. Our first point is this. By this bill, Parliament and through Crown, is breaching the tikanga of our sacred tato pounamu reached between Ngati Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa and Tuhoe in the early 19th century.

A tato pounamu is not a metaphor, it is not folklore, it is a binding peace accord. Sealed in greenstone and in whakapapa, symbolic marriages between our people, and the symbolic marriage of our maunga. That ended generations of conflict and fixed agreed boundaries between our iwi. The Waitangi Tribunal records this tato pounamu into Te Ururu Report Volume 2, at pages 653, 656 to 659, pages 664 to 665, and 693. The supporting record is annexed to our written submission as Appendix A.

When the Crown and Parliament legislates over land that lies on the Kahungunu side of that accord, without our consent, the Crown is not merely making a political mistake, the Crown is breaking the tato pounamu on our behalf, and that is not the Crown's to break. Our second point is the practical one. Where exactly is the tato pounamu boundary?

Our tipuna fixed on the ground, Te Pootu, Kuha, the marae on the left-hand side of Tuai and Tuteropinga are the two anchor points. Mangapapa, roughly halfway between them, is acknowledged as the tribal boundary under tato pounamu. That matters here because the six reserves that this bill proposes to transfer into Te Ururu Board lay on the Kahungunu side of Mangapapa.

They are not on the Tuhoe side, they are not on the Ruapani side, they are on ours. So the bill, in plain effect, takes land that sits on the Kahungunu side of a peace boundary fixed 200 years ago and hands governance of it to a Tuhoe-led board. This is the breach. This is what we are asking the committee to also see.

Our third point is that Mr Christopher Finlayson KC, who is now Deputy Chair of Te Ururu Board, and who was the minister who architected the Tuhoe settlement legislation, said on oath in High Court recording, "I have always considered it was necessary to have a principled historical basis on which to provide redress to a group." We accept that test, we hold the Crown to it, and we say to this committee, there is no principled historical basis for this deal. Appendix A to our written submission sets out the historical record in full, including Chapter 7 of Te Ururu

Volume 2, the four southern blocks at pages 689, 690, the Map 7.2 at page 756, and the events around the Te Hap Te Pe Deed of 1867 and the subsequent Lock Deed of 1872. Both attempts where I feel the Crown in their haste to alienate our whenua has made mistakes that have left lasting grievances for all iwi and hapū involved.

Taken in its totality, Appendix A supports a single conclusion. No principled historical basis exists for transferring these reserves out from under Kahungunu mana o te whenua and into the Te Ururu Board. The tribunal itself, at pages 689 and 619 of Volume 2, found that Kahungunu, Ruapani and Tuhoe all have overlapping customary rights and interests in Te Ururu.

Within our own collective knowledge, oral and written, our tipuna are generally acknowledged as having the dominant mana o te whenua in the southern half of those blocks. Tuhoe and Ruapani, mana o te whenua in the northern half. This bill ignores that distinction. It treats the whole area as if it were Tuhoe Ruapani country. It is not. Our fourth and final point to you, the MPs directly, legal counsel has advised us that over the past 20 to 30 years, a convention has grown. Judges call it the principle of committee, under which enabling

legislation does not fundamentally alter a signed deed of settlement, and select committee should not seek alterations to a deed at this stage. We understand the practice has been bipartisan. We submit with respect that the practice has to stop with this bill. Rubber-stamping deeds is an abdication of your role as lawmakers.

It is all the more so here because the Ruapani submission to this very committee, along with its entity, Te Whare Taupoki submission to the bill, affirms Ngati Ruapani Mai Waikarimana as a hapū of Tuhoe. If Ruapani Mai Waikarimana are a hapū of Tuhoe, this committee has to answer a hard question.

Is the Tuhoe Claims Settlement Act 2014, declared full and final in 2014, no longer full and final? And if it is no longer full and final, is it lawful for Parliament nowTo break the 2014 settlement law by legislating again over the same iwi.

We do note that Ruapani Mai Waikaremoana is not listed as a hapū in the TSA 2014 section 13 part one of schedule one, so perhaps the committee would like to ask what hapū are they listed as. Those are not rhetorical questions. Those are questions the committee, as lawmakers, must answer before reporting this bill back.

To conclude, we submit the following. Firstly, if Ruapani deed of settlement is legislated as it stands, we view the Crown's conduct as dishonorable, that we will no longer regard our own Tatau Tatau o Te Wairoa full and final settlement as binding upon our whānau hapū kāhui. Second, the Crown and Parliament, by passing this bill as it stands, will have acted so dishonorably that as a matter of tikanga, it will have broken the Tatau Pounamu peace accord reached between Kāngungunu and Tūhoe in the early 1800s.

The Crown does not have the authority to do that. Parliament does not have the authority to do that. We respectfully recommend this to the committee. A, pause this bill. B, test the so-called principled historical basis for the six reserves with reference to Te Urureta report volume two, chapter seven. C, report back to the House that the bill in its current form cannot proceed without free, prior, and informed consent.

Or remove the transfer of the six stock reserves and two Te Urureta board from this bill, thus not breaching our Tatau Pounamu, allowing Ngāti Ruapani Mai Waikaremoana to reach settlement and continuing negotiations, wānanga hui between the peoples of Tūhoe, Kāngungunu, Ruapani over their overlapping interests in these six reserves.

The latter being our preferred option. Legal counsel has advised the committee has asked for a solution. We propose- This morning... this morning. We propose an iwi tripartite group, two Ruapani, two Tūhoe seats, two Kāngungunu seats with a five to six majority vote built into the constitution, therefore allowing us not to kick the can down the road and get to the business and organize ourselves and our own governance over our own whenua.

Tena koutou, tena koutou, tena koutou katoa. Kia ora. Go ahead. And thank you very much for the solution that you provided at the end because that was the- Mm... obvious question, because we've heard loud and clear as a committee from a number of submitters, and I'm expecting to hear some more from some after you about the actual problem. We've got to try and find a solution.

And you're right with the fact that the select committee historically has made nominal amendments but nothing of majority amendment. But that doesn't mean that we as a committee can't have conversations with people at be to be able to get an enduring settlement going forward type. So you're right in what you're saying itself.

We're actually out of time because we're on a very tight time schedule, but I'm pretty sure that-... Tatau Tatau and others will be similarly able to back you up. But thank you very much for your submissions. Thank you. Tena koutou. Tena koutou. There's been a slight rearrangement. I understand that this has been agreed by the submitters, so we're moving on to the Erepeti Marae committee, please.

It's this one right here, is it? Oh. Tena koutou. Tena koutou, tena koutou katoa. So you've probably heard my beginning before. We've got 10 minutes here on the front? Yep. Yep, 10 minutes allocated for your time with us. Ka pai. We've heard the opportunity to read what you've provided us, right?

So use that time as you see fit. Ka pai. Thank you very much. Keep my comments brief. Tena koutou katoa. E te komiti. Ko Matukuhia, ko Whakapunake, ko Maungapohatu Ō mātau maunga e tū mai nei. Ko Ruakituri, ko Waioroa, ko Pupu, ko Nengenenge, ko Tangirau ngā awa e rere kau ana ki te moana-nui-a-Kiwa.

Ko Tākitimu te waka. Ko Te Aitanga a Paurangahua, ko Ngāti Hinganga ngā hapū. Ko Erepeti te marae. He uri ahau nō Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa nō Tūhoe hoki. Ko Tina Mehare tōku ingoa. Kia ora, Tina. Ka noho ahau i mua i a koutou he mānga mō tōku whānau, ō mātau hapū anō hoki.

Ehara i te mea mā mātau anake te hunga ora tēnei kōrero, ā, mā ō mātau tūpuna, mā ō mātau reanga kāore anō kia p**a mai. Mm. Mā tātau katoa tēnei kōrero. Erepeti Marae is affiliated to Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa, Tūhoe, Ngāti Rongo Mauwahine, Ngāti Ruapani, and Iwi of Tūranginui a Kiwa.

We are known collectively as Ngā Tokorima o Hinemanuhiri. We are of our hapū, Ngāti Hinganga, Te Aitanga a Paurangahua, Te Pae Rauta, and Ngāti Kōtere. We have made this submission on behalf of trustees and whānau beneficiaries of Erepeti Marae.

Erepeti Marae is represented by our post-settlement governance entity, Tatau Tatau o Te Wairoa Trust. We sit within the kāhui beneficial entity of Te Hononga o Ngā Awa Trust. We are beneficiaries of the settlement agreement between the Crown and the iwi and hapū of Te Rohe o Te Wairoa that was signed in 2016.

At this point, we wish to make it clear that we fully support a settlement for our Ngāti Ruapani whānau and all of the subsequent benefits it will bring to current and future generations of Ngāti Ruapani. We wish our whanaunga every success for a positive outcomeTātou Tātou o Te Wairoa has raised concerns about a specific part of the settlement regarding the decision to transfer certain lands to Te Urewera Board.

Erepeti Marae shares the concern of Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa. I quote from the summary deed of settlement between Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikaremoana and the Crown, "A key aspiration for Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikaremoana is to see Crown owned land in their area of interest returned into Te Urewera where it will be protected for future generations of Ngāti Ruapani."

The Minister for Treaty of Waitangi Negotiations has said that from a legal perspective, the proclamation of one group's interest should not undermine or diminish the interests of another, and that groups have shared interests and particular places are encouraged to work together to find ways to honor one another's interests, and to manage the lands in accordance with each other's wishes.

During the course of the settlement negotiations, Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa and Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikaremoana have been negotiating a co-management agreement of the following whenua and wāhi tapu where there are shared interests. Waikareiti Conservation Area, Ruakituri Scenic Reserve, Pane Kirikiri Conservation Area, Tūtaemaru Conservation Area, Maungaone Conservation Area, Waihi South Conservation Area.

In the November 2016 deed of settlement between Ngāti Kahungunu ki Te Wairoa and the Crown, there were statutory acknowledgments and deeds of recognition that legally recognized our interests in and in connection with specific whenua and wāhi tapu. A number of sites on the list fall within the areas I just mentioned.

Those sites are the Pane Kirikiri Conservation Area, Ruakituri rivers and its tributaries, Wairoa River and its tributaries, Waiau River and its tributaries, Waikaretaheke River and its tributaries, Hungaroa River and its tributaries. Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa and Ngāti Ruapani Waikaremoana, to our knowledge, have not yet come to an agreement regarding a suitable co-management arrangement, and this gives rise to three potential issues. Number one, the transfer of these lands to another entity, particularly Te Urewera, may

change the non-exclusive arrangement to an exclusive one. Our concern is that the interest of hapū and whānau who are represented by other entities, such as Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa, may not be recognized by Te Urewera, and we may be shut out of decision-making and lose our rights to manage and protect our whenua.

Number two, the concern for existing partnerships between the Department of Conservation and Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa. Similar to the first issue, we cannot be sure that Te Urewera can or will recognize these existing partnerships. Number three, if the lands pass into the management of Te Urewera, there is uncertainty around not only access for us, the hapū and whānau with shared interests, but for all New Zealanders.

Therefore, there are three recommendations that Tātou Tātou o Te Wairoa makes as part of this submission, which we support. Those recommendations are, number one, that the Select Committee amend the bill to remove provisions transferring the five conservation areas and one scenic reserve to Te Urewera, or pause the progression of the bill until a satisfactory arrangement to protect overlapping interests has been made.

Number two, that the Select Committee amend the bill to explicitly protect existing partnerships of iwi, hapū, whānau and iwi authorities with the Department of Conservation. Number three, that the bill includes provisions that ensure iwi and hapū of Te Rohe O Wairoa are guaranteed practical and meaningful representation in any governance or management arrangements affecting these lands.

To conclude this submission, we would like to make this committee aware that our people are no strangers to dealings of this kind. For more than 150 years, the Crown has given themselves all the power to decide whose interests matter most and what happens to those who are perceived to be a threat to its power.

It is so obviously an exercise to divide and conquer Ngāti Kahungunu, Ngāti Ruapani and Tūhoe, of which we are all whānau. Since the Ha Te Pe deed was signed in 1867, the block deed after it in 1872, which led to the loss of the four southern blocks, we have been classified friendlies and rebels.

We have had our land taken, reclassified, reallocated, cut up, tiny pieces returned with unmanageable conditions, and the rest stolen from us, leaving us economically, socially, and culturally destitute and divided. We have tried to fight for our land to be returned, doing so at huge risk of losing further land and accruing crippling debt.

Like Tūhoe Ngāti Ruapani tried to do in 1875 when they asked the Native Land Court to intervene when Crown grants were given to Ngāti Kahungunu as a result of the block deed. They did it to try and protect their interests, which would be inherited by their future generations. This is exactly what we are doing today. Let me be clear, this submission is not in opposition to Ngāti Ruapani, nor is it in opposition to Tūhoe. Rather, it is in opposition to the law that is removing our rights to our whenua.The Crown has consistently maintained over the

years, including during the evidence to the Waitangi Tribunal during their Turuwera inquiry, that no prejudice arose from their dealings and no breaches of Te Tiriti o Waitangi occurred. Yet, by 1872, we found ourselves landless and pitted against one another for our rights and our interests to be recognized. We find ourselves here today in 2026 trying to stop the same thing happening yet again.

We are tired of having these conversations, and the Crown must be so tired of listening to all of these voices and dissent of opposition, of spending years, decades, millions of dollars negotiating agreements that are impossible for us to agree to. Give us back our rangatiratanga. Honour Te Tiriti o Waitangi so our future generations never have to fight like we and our ancestors had to.

Ka reira atu te mihi kia o ngā whare o ngā whānau o te marae o Erepeeti ki te komiti nei. We thank the committee for their consideration of our submissions. Tena koutou katoa. Kia ora. Thank you very much. Another good submission, and you've made it very clear what you believe is the solution as well, and that's important for us to understand that. Unfortunately, we don't have enough time for any questions.
But thank you very much for your submission. Kia ora. Ka pai. Nga mihi nui ki a koe. So next up is Ngati Pahauwera Development Trust. They're online? Okay.

Tena koe. Tena koe. Tena ratou katoa. Nga iwi, nga hapu i waenganui i era whare o Parramatta. My name is Toru Waaka. I'm the chairperson of Ngati Pahauwera. Our submission is made on behalf of our iwi, the Ngati Pahauwera Development Trust, in relation to the above bill, inasmuch as it affects Waikaremoana, associated lands and reserves, and related government governance arrangements.

We support the settlement of historical treaty claims and acknowledge the aspirations of Ngati Ruapani by Waikaremoana to achieve redress. Our submission is not opposed to settlement. One of the reasons we don't oppose it is that

Ruapani is very much a part of us, as are many other relationships. Ruapani himself died on my land at Moaka, defending our collective interests. And also the patu pounamu that was referred to in one of the Wairoa submissions. That was a patu pounamu passed over by one of our rangatira, Tureia, who was a tipuna of Ngati Pahauwera. As I say, we are very much connected to Ngati Ruapani, and we do want to see them

achieve a success in terms of their settlement. Our concerns are narrow and principled. One of the reasons I say that is we, in the past, have had agreements regarding the Crown. One I specifically refer to is the Mangaha Rurutangi to Trust settlement, whereby much of our lands were given away as part of that settlement. We lost lots of our interests.

So a reference to the fact that we do not want a settlement that creates new grievances for the people of Te Wairoa is a very pertinent one. Ngati Kahungunu, in supporting the settlement, we do not want to create foreseeable treaty grievances, but we want to secure durable settlement outcomes.

Ngati Pahauwera, along with the other hapu of Ngati Kahungunu, we recognize Lake Waikaremoana was a traditional food source shared by many iwi including Ngati Pahauwera and many of our members, and also shareholders in the Waikaremoana lands.

One of our members, Huriana Lawrence, he put in one of the earliest tribunal claims to the Waikaremoana Trust lands, and I am disappointed with the fact that he has been left out of this process. His tipuna was a owner of much of the lands in that area.

The Ngati Pahauwera traditional area of interest follows the Wairoa up to towards Lake Waikaremoana. We did utilize it as part of our food gathering areas like many others. Ngati Pahauwera also acknowledges the Kahungunu whakapapa through Hinemanuhiri and her descendants.

Ae, toko rima o Hinemanuhiri, Tamatarangi, Makoro, Hingarangi, Pupuni and Pareora. Pareora is one of our tipuna associated with our Putiri marae, which has also strong relationships with the Waikaremoana area.Ngāti Kahungunu exercise customary authority across Lake Waikaremoana and Waikareti.

The four southern blocks, the Waiau, Tukurangi, Taramarama and Rukuturi, and the Waiau, Waikaretaheke, Rukuturi, Hangaroa and Wairoa River systems. These interests are substantial, but not exclusive, exercised alongside Ngāti Ruapani and Ngāi Tūhoe, and function as an interconnected landscape governed through whakapapa, seasonal movement, mahinga kai, wāhi tapu and negotiated hapū relationships.

As demonstrated in the Crown Commissioner research of Belgrave and Young, Māori customary rights in Waikaremoana operated as negotiated spaces, not fixed or exclusive boundaries. Attempts to impose a single iwi narrative retrospectively distorts the historic reality.

Fighting of the courts. Multiple forums addressing Waikaremoana have been in play over the past with the Crown, and they've been unable to reconcile competing claims precisely because each claiming group held legitimate ancestral rights. In its consideration of the bed of Lake Waikaremoana, the Native Land Court expressly found that each of the three contending parties had ancestral rights, and that proposed boundary lines asserted by any party could not be corrected

in their entirety. Limits to the inquiry. The Waitangi Tribunal acknowledged that the division of inquiry districts between Te Urewera and Te Wairoa constrained the ability of Ngāti Kahungunu and Ngāi Tamanutarangi to fully present evidence relating to Waikaremoana.

The Crown practice and risk of government monopoly. The historical record demonstrates a consistent Crown pattern favoring singular governance arrangements for administrative convenience. To me, it's like a paint-by-numbers process just to get settlements over the line to end the settlement process.

It tends to ignore overlapping customary interests and, like I said, with the Maungaharuru Tangi Te Trust and the Henare settlement, our lands were given to compensate other iwi interests at our expense. Non-exclusive redress must operate in practice.

The Crown has repeatedly asserted that redress in this area is non-exclusive. We accept non-exclusivity in principle. However, there needs to be clear performance measures in the management agreement arrangements for the claimants to be able to monitor whether this is actually put into practice.

History demonstrates that such outcomes predictably generate process, litigation, and long-term grievance, and yes, so it needs to be done within the constraints of tikanga as well as the law. The specific concerns arising under the bill, they relate to six Department of Conservation areas as well.
A Maungoni Conservation Area, Panekirikiri Conservation Area, the Tutuaemaru Conservation Area, Hawaiki South Conservation Area, the Waikareti Conservation Area, and the Rukuturi Scenic Reserve.

Toro, can we bring your submission to a close soon? Because we're running out of time. Can you make some closing remarks, please? Yes. So as I repeat, our submission does not seek to undermine the Ruapani settlement. It seeks to ensure that Parliament does not resolve one grievance by creating another one.

And yeah, as I referred to, we are a victim of that process, Ngati Pari, already. The historic record between the committee is clear. Waikaremoana has never been exclusive. Durable settlements require legitimate governance. And as I said earlier, it would be good to have some monitoring and performance measure arrangements in the co-management agreements of these conservation reserves so that people can have a real input in terms of managing.

Ngati Pari has a co-management agreement with DOC, and it is definitely lacking in terms of where we get input. So in my final statement, durable settlements require legitimate governance, inclusive decision-making, and a respect for established relationships grounded on whakapapa and tikanga. Tena ra tautoko.

Tena koe, Toro, and you've been very clear with your submission, both in writing as well as verbally. Unfortunately, we are out of time, but we appreciate what you've brought before us today. So thank you, Toro. Kia ora. Kia ora koutou. Okay, we're going to move to Tatotato now.There are 13 copies in the sleeve for your pleasure.

Tena koe. Tena koe. And once again, thank you for the very fulsome submission that you provided with all the evidence that you've got here. You've done a great job with that. We've got 10 minutes here, as you've heard before, so please use it as you see fit. Kia ora. All going well, there should be one minute in reserve. Okay.

You fire away, because we are short on time. Thank you. He te hear mana whanau McLeod, maunga Titohea, tena koe. Kai nga mema o te committee whakawa, tena koutou katua. Kai aku rangatira, kai nga whanaunga, e Ngati Ruapani. Ka mihi kau ana ki o koutou. He tikanga no tenei what, kia huri mai taku tura ki o koutou aroaro mai.

Kia ora. My name is Pirri Munro, and I am the chair of Tatau Tatau o te Wairoa Post-Settlement Governance Entity. With me today is a small roopu tautoko who have traveled here especially for this day in support of our submission. We appear on behalf of the iwi and hapu o te rohe o te Wairoa, Ngati Kahungunu ki te Wairoa. At the outset, I want to be clear about our position. We support treaty settlements.

We support Ngati Ruapani achieving settlement. But we oppose elements of the bill that transfer or effectively place six Department of Conservation reserves under arrangements that marginalize other mana whenua and create a foreseeable future grievance. This submission is deliberately narrow. It is not about the whole bill.

It is about six specific reserves and how Parliament avoids repeating past mistakes. We are also mindful of the context before this committee. There have been 81 submissions on this bill. Independent analysis shows 59 opposing and 22 supporting. That tells us this bill is contested and not consensus. In that environment, we are looking for two things, clarity about what opposition is targeting and practical amendment pathways that reduce future conflict.

Our submission is designed to do exactly that. We are not opposing settlement, we are identifying specific flexible risk. Our position is grounded first and foremost in whakapapa. The iwi and hapu of te rohe o te Wairoa hold deep intergenerational connections to Waikaremoana and the surrounding lands, including all six reserves affected by this bill.

Our whakapapa is inseparable from Ngati Ruapani, but it is also distinct and enduring. These connections flow through Kahungunu and Omaiwahine, their son Kahukuranui, his daughter Hinemanuhiri, and her union with Pukaru, the son of Ruapani. From that union came Ngatokirimane Manuhiri, Tamaterangi, Makoro, Hinganga, Pupuni, and Pareora, the tipuna of many hapu of te rohe o te Wairoa.

These are not abstract genealogies. They are lived and continuous. In the Waiau, Waikaretaheke, Ruakituri, Hangaroa, and Wairoa Rivers, in mahinga kai sites, in wahi tapu and urupa, and in the ongoing practices of ahi ka, ahi komau, and kaitiakitanga. The key point is simple.

These lands have never belonged culturally or historically to one iwi alone. The hapu of te rohe o te Wairoa with recognized interests include Ngai Tamaterangi, Ngati Makoro, Ngati Hinganga, Ngati Hinemanuhiri, Ngati Mihi, Ngati Matangirau, Ngai Te Kawae and Ngati Putere, Ngai Kohatu, Ngati Hinehika, Ngati Hinanga.

These relationships have been maintained through occupation, mahinga kai, cultural practice, and continued return. Where a permanent occupation was disrupted was due to Crown actions, not abandonment. That distinction matters. Submissions before you are not one single block of opposition. They fall into distinct streams. Some support the bill as a long overdue settlement. Some, like us, support settlement but raise durability risks, particularly where a non-exclusive redress may become exclusive in practice.

Others raise boundary and mana whenua concerns from different directions. The implication is very clear. If the drafting is not precise, Parliament risks embedding conflict from multiple directions and not resolving it. Ministers has described this redress as non-exclusive. We accept that principle, but we say this clearly.

Non-exclusive redress must operate in practice, not just in theory. If governance arrangements remove us from decision-making, override existing partnerships, or place control in a body where we have no seat, then redress becomes exclusive in practice, regardless of what the legislation says. That is not durable settlement.

Our concern is targeted and practical. It relates to six reserves. Mangaonea, Panekirikiri, Tutaimaro, Waihi South, Waikareiti, Ruakituri. These six reserves are where the risk is most acute, and importantly, this is where the bill can be amended. This is not a theoretical issue. It is a precise drafting issue with a clear solution.

Through our settlement and longstanding relationships, Te Rohe o Te Wairoa has established partnerships with the Department of Conservation Te Papa Atawhai. These arrangements recognize our interest and were intended to give effect to non-exclusive relationships. However, we have not been engaged by the Minister of Conservation. We have not been consulted on the proposed changes affecting these reserves. This is despite obligations under our deed of settlement. That failure matters.

Parliament should not endorse changes to conservation land governance that undermine partnership commitments or proceed without engagement with affected iwi. There is also a broader lesson. The proposed arrangements would place these lands under governance where effective control sits with one iwi, and Te Rohe o Te Wairoa has no representation. The 2024 report by the Office of the Auditor-General found that Tutuweta has not been effectively managed and highlighted ongoing funding and delivery challenges.

That is a warningIt shows what happens when governance arrangements are not durable, inclusive, and properly supported. If those risks are repeated here, it will not just affect iwi, it will affect the public. If conservation areas are not well-maintained and there is a mishap in terms of safety, people will stop visiting.

That has implications for public access, tourism, and New Zealand's wider reputation and economy. It is the minister who has amplified the point of public access. These risks are real and avoidable. The Auditor-General has also repeatedly warned that poor settlement implementation, weak governance legitimacy, and ignored overlap risks lead directly to litigation, compensation, and loss of confidence that settlements are indeed full and final.

In a Te Tari Whakamo memo to the minister, dated 22 April 2025, and in part, it states their relevant policy position. Quote, "Crown overlapping interest policy guides you on what you should consider when making decisions on contested redress. The policy includes the principles of, A, partnership. Māori and the Crown have a duty to act reasonably and in good faith towards each other. B, active participation.

The Crown seeks to ensure it actively protects the interests of all groups, including overlapping groups and the settling group, and to actively preserve and promote amicable relations with and between groups. And C, redress. The Crown needs to understand how its actions might affect the interest of a claimant group and the interest of overlapping groups.

In doing so, the Crown must be conscious of the need to avoid the creation of fresh injustice and the need to maintain capacity to provide appropriate redress in future negotiations." We say respectfully, Parliament should not legislate unforeseeable grievance. Our ask is practical and it's proportionate.

We ask the committee to recommend that the bill be amended to remove the six identified reserves from arrangements that diminish the rights and interests of Te Rohe to Wairoa, or pause those aspects of the bill until overlapping interests are properly resolved, and ensure non-exclusive redress is made real through enforceable safeguards, and protect existing Department of Conservation partnership arrangements, and require meaningful engagement, including by the Minister of Conservation, before any reserve governance changes proceed.

Might I add to this, Chair, in listening to the questions and your seek for solutions. If you were to expedite this, your question was about time, then you might expedite that after having removed these for consultation and engagement within the respective parties to bring in a commissioner, an appointed commissioner, to expedite that process.

This submission is not about stopping a settlement. It is about ensuring that in resolving one grievance, Parliament does not create the next. Durable settlements require inclusion, legitimacy, and good faith implementation. That is what we are asking you to protect. Therefore, Chair and members of the select committee, to my chiefs of Ngati Ruapani, to all my relatives. Thank you. Thank you. Goodbye.

Let us all meet again. Thank you. I will allow, does anybody have one short question? I think it's very clear what you see the issue is and what you see as potentially some of the solutions are. So unless there's no questions, hey, thank you very much, Piripi. We appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
Okay, we're going to ask for Te Pari Taipoki to please now come forward.

Welcome. Thank you. So again, thank you very much. So this provides you the opportunity of responding really to what you've been hearing. And at the end of the day, clearly we have questions in our heads as the committee and that, but please help us to understand where you are positioned with us and what you've heard.

It'd be good to help us with our understanding itself. So tena koe, Kara. Kia ora. Kia ora koutou. I'll keep this short and sweet so that it gives you an opportunity to ask questions. Thank you. I want to leave the committee with five things. First, what this bill is. This bill gives legal effect to a deed of settlement that has been negotiated in good faith over many years.

The historical account is true. The apology is owed. The redress is hard won, and we ask Parliament to honor the agreement the Crown has made with us and to pass the bill without amendment. Second, cultural redress. The transfer of lands into Te Ruawera is not a transfer into private ownership.

It is recognition of what has always been. The Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana holds a responsibility of kaitiaki in this rohe through whakapapa and as ahika. Te Ruawera as a legal entity is the appropriate framework for that authority to be exercised.

The sites returned to the Okahu kaitaua and other places are for our whanau to come home to. Third, and I want to speak plainly to this. On the concerns raised by Ngati Kahungunu, Ngati Pahauwera. We have heard the submissions both here, in our negotiations, and at home on our marae at Waikaremoana.

Mm-hmm. We ask the committee to hold three things in their minds. One, those who whakapapa to Waikaremoana will always whakapapa to Waikaremoana.That is a matter of tikanga, that is a matter of kawa, not of statute, and it is not affected by this bill.

Let's not conflate whakapapa with entities. Two, those PSGEs who have submitted today, Katokomihatukikou Pou, have already settled with the Crown. They've settled with you. Their settlements are full and final. They had the opportunity, through their own negotiations, to seek redress in respect of the lands they have noted, and the outcomes of that process are what they are.

And we see and we recognize that those entities have deeds of recognition, statutory acknowledgments in places which we would say are within our area. But most importantly on this point, our settlement is not the vehicle for revisiting theirs.

And the third point here, Kahungunu via the Tatoutatou o te Wairua deed of settlement also have a pathway available to them through their own settlement arrangements in respect of that Te Urewera board. That avenue exists. It is the proper one for them to exercise their interests.

Fourth, on Tūhoe. Our relationship with Tūhoe is one of whanaungatanga. It is whakapapa, not administrative. Tūhoe have supported the settlement, and the support reflects a shared understanding of who holds responsibility in this rohe. Those who have committed the resources, the time, and the effort for the care and stewardship of our rohe.

That living relationship will continue to guide how Waikaremoana and Te Urewera are cared for, regardless of what is written in any act of Parliament. Fifth, on the cost of delaying, our kaumatua have waited their whole lives. Each year of delay deepens the disconnection from our rohe, from one another, from the future of our rangatahi deserve to inherit. No financial figure compensates for what has been taken, but this bill is the first step. It lets the rebuilding begin.

There is no benefit for the Crown, for our people, or for the relationship across this rohe in prolonging this further. Our request remains simple, that the bill is recommended to be passed without amendment. Acknowledge in your report the significance of the historical record and the Crown's apologies, and prioritize its passage.

We are ready, our governance is in place, and our people are waiting. Thank you. I've got a very simple question. At the end of the day, what I've heard over the occasion we had up at Tuai and also today is basically your whanaungatanga. You have different entities that you represent when you come before us and talk to it, and it's all in passion plays itself.

But I'm always thinking personally, is that my part in a treaty settlement process here with my fellow committee members, is it fair and reasonable? And also, is it something that as you're committing to, are you happy to live with what it is with your whanaungatanga, with everything like that?

So my question is about that. You're wanting us to commit and proceed as is type thing. Clearly, we've got others that don't believe that's the right way. There should be a part of it that should be looked at differently to what it is. At the end of the day, if this passes through the third reading and goes to royal assent, you have to live with it.

So my question is, is that what you're happy with? Is that where you feel right to be positioned with it? I guess just to reiterate my last point, we are ready, our governance is in place, and our people are waiting. Yes. Appreciate that. You've got the structure. That's mechanical stuff largely. I'm talking about the fact is this sets a beginning, a rebuild of the future is what settlements are all about, and it sets in place that for you. And Eugene, I know you've been involved with this for a long time. I'm not trying to be pointed in my

question. It's just a fair question. You have to live with what the settlement is, and you think that's very fair and just. I want you to be sure about that. So the question is about, are you comfortable with where we're at, considering what you've heard from the wider community, just down the road and your whanaungatanga from elsewhere?

That's the question I'm asking. Yeah, we've heard them say they support the settlement. It's just the land blocks that we propose don't- Yeah... seem to do it. Mm-hmm. But we're always ready to sit down and talk. That's how we started this latest run in the Ruapani claims, was by sitting down and talking with Tūhoe.

Right. Finding a way forward that we can both support. As I think I heard one or two of them say, to move forward, we have to sit down and talk. And I think we're committed to doing that after we settle. Yeah. Okay. Committee members, any questions?

If I may just say- Go ahead... we can hear the pain and frustrations and the way that you just shared your kōrero right now, your submission. Look, I thank you for that. It's clear to me that you're all family, and that's how you're going to go forward with this.

Okay. So the process from here is that we go into deliberation. So we get the opportunity to ask more questions of officials and others, including the minister if we choose to, about proceeding through here. I say again, I'd hate to think that we're creating a problem for you in this room here. You've all been very polite in your submissions to one another and that.

I'd like to think relationships are not torn apart as a result of a treaty settlement. We're just trying to repair the wrongs of the past type thing. So, we have a job to do, and we thank you very much for the input of everybody involved in the submissions here. I see some of the submitters from Tūhoe as well.

And it's been a wonderful occasion for us to learn more about your place, about your whānau, and about your history, and that. So yes, we have the next step to take, and aroha mai, thank you for the long journeys that many of you have taken, and a safe journey home from today. Kia ora mai. Kia ora.
